speaker: patient
text: Hi, Doctor. I’ve been feeling really tired and thirsty lately, and I’ve been urinating more often than usual.

speaker: doctor
text: Thanks for coming in. Let me check your vitals — your systolic blood pressure today is 131 mmHg.

speaker: patient
text: That’s higher than my usual reading at home. I usually see around 120, but I’ve been stressed this week.

speaker: doctor
text: Your heart rate is elevated at 126 bpm — that’s quite tachycardic. Are you experiencing palpitations or shortness of breath?

speaker: patient
text: Yes, especially when I walk up stairs. And my vision’s been a little blurry the past few days.

speaker: doctor
text: Based on your symptoms and initial labs, I’m diagnosing you with Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus.

speaker: doctor
text: Your hemoglobin is 168 g/L — that’s within normal range, so no anemia concern.

speaker: doctor
text: Your blood glucose is significantly elevated at 14.4 mmol/L — well above the fasting target of under 7.0.

speaker: doctor
text: You’re currently on Atorvastatin 40mg at night for cholesterol management — we’ll continue that for now.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve been checking my blood sugar at home with my glucometer — it’s been running high all week.

speaker: doctor
text: How are you managing meals and activity? Any recent changes in diet or weight?

speaker: patient
text: I lost about 4 kg unintentionally over the last month, even though I’m eating more — especially carbs.

speaker: doctor
text: That weight loss despite increased appetite is classic for uncontrolled diabetes. We’ll need to start glucose-lowering therapy urgently.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve also had some numbness in my toes — just mild, but it’s new.

speaker: doctor
text: We’ll assess for neuropathy at your next visit. For now, let’s get your glucose under control — I’m prescribing metformin 500 mg twice daily, starting today.

speaker: patient
text: Should I still take my Atorvastatin?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes — keep taking Atorvastatin 40mg at night as prescribed. It’s important for cardiovascular protection in diabetes.

speaker: patient
text: I checked my blood sugar first thing this morning before breakfast — it was 14.4 again.

speaker: doctor
text: That confirms persistent hyperglycemia. We’ll monitor closely and adjust dosing if needed after two weeks.

speaker: patient
text: Right — that number matches the result from last time.
